issues.evidence:
base = div(amount, n)
    remainder = rem(amount, n)

    Enum.map(0..(n - 1), fn i ->
      cents = if i < remainder, do: base + 1, else: base
issues.why: For a negative amount `rem/2` returns a negative remainder (e.g. amount -5, n 3: base -1, remainder -2), so `i < remainder` is never true and all shares stay -1, summing to -3 instead of -5 — directly violating the prompt's guarantee that shares 'always sum back to the original amount' even though the struct explicitly says amount 'may be negative'.

The sum-back invariant is only pinned for non-negative amounts, so the negative-amount split defect above passes the harness unnoticed, leaving a boundary the prompt implies ('may be negative') unverified.

issues.evidence:
base = div(amount, n)
    remainder = rem(amount, n)

    Enum.map(0..(n - 1), fn i ->
      cents = if i < remainder, do: base + 1, else: base
issues.why: For negative amounts `rem/2` is negative (e.g. amount -1000, n 3 gives base -333, remainder -1), so `i < remainder` is never true and every share equals base: [-333, -333, -333] sums to -999, not -1000 — directly violating the prompt's guarantee that "the shares always sum back to the original amount" for amounts the prompt says "may be negative".

issues.evidence:
base = div(amount, n)
    remainder = rem(amount, n)

    Enum.map(0..(n - 1), fn i ->
      cents = if i < remainder, do: base + 1, else: base
issues.why: For a negative amount, Elixir's rem/2 returns a result with the same sign as the dividend (e.g. rem(-1000, 3) == -1), so `remainder` is negative and `i < remainder` is false for every i in 0..(n-1); no share ever receives the +1 adjustment, so e.g. Money.split(Money.new(-1000, :JPY), 3) yields three shares of -333 that sum to -999, not -1000, directly violating the prompt's 'the shares always sum back to the original amount' requirement for a value the struct explicitly allows (amount 'may be negative').

issues.evidence: name = Keyword.get(opts, :name, __MODULE__)
issues.why: The prompt lists explicit defaults for :pool_size (3), :max_queue (10), and :promote_after_ms (5_000) but states no default for :name, merely calling it 'an option for process registration' — implying that omitting it should start an anonymous, pid-addressed process. Defaulting instead to the module atom means a second `PriorityWorkerPool.start_link(...)` without :name in the same node returns `{:error, {:already_started, pid}}` instead of starting an independent pool, a materially different and surprising contract that the harness never exercises because every test supplies an explicit unique :name.

issues.evidence: cancel-running branch: `new_cancelled = MapSet.put(state.cancelled_refs, ref)` ... `Process.exit(worker_pid, :kill)` ... `new_state = %{state | cancelled_refs: new_cancelled, cancelled_count: state.cancelled_count + 1}` (worker is NOT removed from `busy_workers`), combined with task_finished: `{^ref, client_pid} -> send(client_pid, {ref, :result, result}); {:noreply, dispatch_next(state, worker)}`
issues.why: Because the cancel handler leaves the worker in `busy_workers`, if a running task completes at the instant it is cancelled the manager may process the worker's already-queued `:task_finished` after the cancel; it then calls `dispatch_next` on the just-killed pid, sending `{:run, ...}` to a dead process so the next queued task is silently dropped and that task's awaiter wrongly receives `{:error, {:task_crashed, reason}}` from the subsequent `:DOWN`. Message ordering between the worker and the cancelling client is not guaranteed, so this concurrency path the prompt implies (cancel a running task, replacement picks up next queued task) can lose work; the blocking-task harness never lets a running task finish during cancel, so it is unpinned.

issues.evidence:
{worker_pid, {^ref, client_pid}} ->
            # Mark this ref as cancelled so the :DOWN handler knows
            new_cancelled = MapSet.put(state.cancelled_refs, ref)
            # Kill the worker — this will trigger :DOWN
            Process.exit(worker_pid, :kill)
issues.why: If a task finishes naturally at nearly the same instant a client calls cancel/2 on that same ref, and the cancel message happens to be processed first (both are just messages in the manager's mailbox, so ordering is not guaranteed), the subsequently-processed `handle_info({:task_finished, ...})` still runs `dispatch_next(state, worker)` and hands the now-dying `worker` a freshly-dequeued task; that `{:run, task}` send is silently lost because the worker is being killed, and the `:DOWN` handler then reports the *new* task's ref as `{:task_crashed, :killed}` (since only the original ref was added to `cancelled_refs`) even though the new task's function body never ran — a queued task can be silently dropped and mis-reported as a crash purely due to message-arrival ordering, a scenario the harness's gated (blocking_task/crash_on_signal) tests are specifically designed to avoid triggering.

issues.evidence:
# Removing the service from the map is sufficient: any in-flight
    # {:check, name} message will hit the :error branch in handle_info
    # and be silently discarded.
    {:reply, :ok, %{state | services: Map.delete(state.services, name)}}
issues.why: This invariant is false once the same service_name is re-registered before the stale {:check, name} message from the OLD registration arrives: handle_info's Map.fetch will now succeed against the NEW service record, so the stale message runs service.check_func.() early and calls schedule_check(name, service.interval_ms) again, spawning a second, independent timer chain that runs concurrently with the legitimate one from the new registration. From that point on the service is checked roughly twice as often as interval_ms specifies (two overlapping recurring timers), silently violating the prompt's 'interval_ms is how often to run the check' contract — and this exact deregister-then-re-register sequence is a scenario the module explicitly supports (there is even a passing test for re-registration).

issues.evidence:
test "can re-register a service after deregistering it", %{mon: mon} do
    check = CheckFn.build("web")
    Monitor.register(mon, "web", check, 1_000)
    Monitor.deregister(mon, "web")
    assert :ok = Monitor.register(mon, "web", check, 1_000)

    assert {:ok, %{status: :pending}} = Monitor.status(mon, "web")
  end
issues.why: This test only checks the status immediately after re-registration; it never lets the original (pre-deregister) timer message actually arrive against the re-registered service, so the duplicate-timer-chain bug described above (stale message from the old registration hitting the new registration of the same name) is never exercised or pinned by the harness, even though deregister/re-register of the same name is a directly implied edge case from the prompt's 'cancels its scheduled checks' requirement.

issues.evidence:
format_errors =
      if not is_nil(value) and is_binary(value) and String.trim(value) != "" and format != nil do
        check_format(String.trim(value), format, field.name)
issues.why: The prompt states 'Format checks only apply to string-typed fields' (i.e. gated on the field's declared `:type`), but the gold gates the check on `is_binary(value)` — the runtime shape of the decoded JSON value — instead of `field.type == :string`. For a field declared with a non-string `:type` (e.g. `:integer`) that also carries a `:format`, a JSON value that happens to be a string (already an independent type error) will additionally trigger a spurious 'does not match expected format' error that the prompt's rule says should never fire for a non-string-typed field, producing a silent extra error a student would wrongly learn is correct.

issues.evidence:
Enum.reduce(fields, {record, vault}, fn field, {rec, v} ->
            case Map.fetch(rec, field) do
              {:ok, value} ->
                {token, v2} = get_or_create_token(v, field, value)
                {Map.put(rec, field, token), v2}
issues.why: The fold over `fields` re-reads the already-mutated `rec` on each step, so if the same field atom appears more than once in `fields` (e.g. `[:email, :email]`), the second pass tokenizes the token produced by the first pass instead of the original value. For record `%{email: "a@x.com"}` with `fields = [:email, :email]`, the final value becomes `"TOK_EMAIL_2"` mapping to `"TOK_EMAIL_1"` in the vault, not to `"a@x.com"`; `detokenize/2` only does one non-recursive lookup, so it returns `"TOK_EMAIL_1"` instead of the original, breaking the prompt's explicit lossless round-trip guarantee. Nothing in the prompt requires `fields` entries to be unique, and the harness never exercises a repeated field name.

`vault.reverse` is a single global map keyed only by the token string (derived from the uppercased field name), not scoped per field atom. Two distinct field atoms that uppercase to the same string (e.g. `:Email` and `:email` in `fields = [:Email, :email]`) each independently start their per-field counter at 1 and both mint `"TOK_EMAIL_1"`; the second field's `reverse` entry silently overwrites the first's, so `detokenize/2` returns the wrong original value for one of the fields. This directly contradicts the prompt's promise that 'Distinct fields produce tokens in distinct namespaces even for equal values,' and the harness has no test with case-colliding or duplicate-derived field namespaces to catch it.

issues.evidence:
LRUCache.put(c, nil, nil)
...
assert {:ok, nil} = LRUCache.get(c, nil)
issues.why: The prompt explicitly requires that a stored `false` be returned as a hit ('including values like `nil` or `false` — presence is decided by the key existing, never by the value being truthy'), but the harness only ever exercises a `nil` value and never stores/reads `false`, so a solution that special-cased `false` (e.g. a truthiness guard distinct from nil-handling) would not be caught; the gold is correct, but this specific boundary is unpinned.

The prompt states "`player_id` can be any term (string, integer, atom)", but the head pattern embeds the raw player_id term unescaped into an ETS match spec. If player_id is one of ETS's reserved match-spec atoms (e.g. `:_`, `:"$1"`, `:"$2"`, ...), the pattern is interpreted as a wildcard/bind-variable rather than a literal key, so `:ets.select_replace/2` can match rows belonging to other players; since select_replace requires the replacement's key to equal the matched object's key, this raises a `badarg` exception instead of returning `:ok` whenever another player's row is also matched, violating the documented "Always return `:ok`" contract and crashing the caller for a class of inputs the prompt explicitly allows.

issues.evidence: :ets.new(table_name, [:set, :named_table, :public, read_concurrency: true])
issues.why: The prompt states 'All writes (enable, disable, set_variants) must go through the GenServer via call to serialise updates,' but marking the table :public (instead of the default :protected, which already permits reads from any process) lets any external process bypass the GenServer entirely and write directly to the table, silently undermining the stated serialisation invariant; a student could wrongly learn that :public is required for cross-process reads when :protected already suffices.

issues.evidence:
defp safe(action, context) do
    case action.(context) do
      {:ok, _} = ok -> ok
      {:error, _} = err -> err
      other -> {:error, {:unexpected_return, other}}
    end
  rescue
    exception -> {:error, {:exception, exception, __STACKTRACE__}}
  end
issues.why: The prompt only specifies that action_fn 'returns {:ok, result} or {:error, reason}' and reserves raise/rescue handling explicitly for compensate_fn ('A raising compensating function must not abort the remaining compensations; catch and record it.'); the gold silently also rescues exceptions from action_fn and normalizes any other return shape into {:error, {:unexpected_return, other}}, an undocumented extra behavior the harness never exercises, so a student could wrongly learn that action functions must always be wrapped in rescue/catch-all logic even though the prompt never asked for that.

issues.evidence: assert {:error, _} = BackoffDLQ.retry(dlq, "q", id, fn _ -> raise "kaboom" end)
issues.why: The prompt explicitly calls out a third failure mode distinct from `{:error, reason}` and a raised exception: 'any other return' (e.g. a handler returning `:weird` or `{:foo, :bar}`) must also be classified as a failure per `run_handler`'s `other -> {:failure, {:unexpected_return, other}}` branch in solution.ex; no test in the harness exercises a handler returning something other than `:ok`, `{:ok, _}`, `{:error, _}`, or a raise/throw, so an implementation that mishandled this branch (e.g. mistakenly treating an arbitrary non-error return as success) would pass the full suite undetected.

issues.evidence:
state = put_queue(%{state | next_id: id + 1}, queue, entries ++ [entry])
...
def handle_call({:peek, queue, count}, _from, state) do
    entries = state.queues |> Map.get(queue, []) |> Enum.take(count) |> Enum.map(&public/1)
    {:reply, entries, state}
  end
issues.why: peek() never sorts by the entry's first_seen field — it relies entirely on list-insertion order to approximate chronological order. The prompt's :clock option is just 'a zero-arity function returning the current time in milliseconds' with no stated monotonicity guarantee, so a caller-supplied clock that ever returns a smaller value than a prior call (e.g. a wall-clock subject to adjustment) would produce a peek ordering that no longer matches 'oldest-first by first_seen', silently violating the documented ordering contract in a way the harness (which only ever advances its fake clock monotonically) cannot catch.

issues.evidence: A `timeout` of `0` returns `{:error, :timeout}` immediately.
issues.why: The gold handles this via the `timeout == 0 -> {:reply, {:error, :timeout}, state}` branch, but the harness only ever exercises timeouts of 20/50ms (`checkout(:vp_basic, 20)`, `checkout(:vp_crash, 50)`), so a regression making timeout=0 block instead of returning immediately would pass every test.

issues.why: Every waiter-related test enqueues at most one blocked caller; there is no test with two or more simultaneous waiters verifying the 'longest-waiting' FIFO ordering the prompt calls out ('handed to the longest-waiting one'), so the queue ordering logic in `deliver`/`:queue` is exercised but never actually pinned.

issues.evidence: assert {:error, :timeout} = OverflowPool.checkout(:op_grow, 50)
issues.why: The prompt makes the zero-timeout path an explicit contract ("A `timeout` of `0` returns `{:error, :timeout}` immediately."), and the gold handles it with a dedicated `timeout == 0 -> {:reply, {:error, :timeout}, state}` branch, but every harness checkout that expects a timeout uses a nonzero value (50 ms here and elsewhere); the zero-timeout boundary is never exercised, so a solution that instead enqueues a 0-timeout caller (blocking indefinitely under `GenServer.call(..., :infinity)`) would pass the suite undetected.

issues.evidence: assert {:error, :timeout} = OverflowPool.checkout(:op_grow, 50)
issues.why: The prompt explicitly names a distinct boundary behavior — "A `timeout` of `0` returns `{:error, :timeout}` immediately" — which the gold implements via a separate `timeout == 0 ->` cond clause that skips monitoring/enqueueing entirely, but no test anywhere in the harness ever calls `checkout` with a literal `0` timeout (all timeout-related tests use 50, 100, 1000, or 5000ms, which exercise the blocking waiter/timer path instead). A regression that mishandled the zero-timeout case (e.g. always enqueuing a waiter even for timeout=0, turning it into a near-instant blocking wait rather than the specified immediate synchronous rejection) would pass every test in this suite.

The :fire message carries only the key, not the timer ref, and cancel_timer's return value is never checked. Process.cancel_timer/1 can return false while the old timer's message is still in flight (already fired but not yet enqueued); that stray {:fire, key} message will later be matched by handle_info/2 against the *new* (reset) burst's state — Map.pop(state, key) does not verify the message corresponds to the currently-installed timer ref — causing the trailing func to fire immediately, before the freshly reset delay_ms has elapsed. This directly violates the prompt's explicit guarantee, 'Trailing executions must not run before delay_ms has elapsed since the most recent call/4 for that key.' The race is timing-dependent (BEAM scheduling jitter around the exact cancellation instant) so it cannot be deterministically pinned by the given harness, but it is a real soundness gap beyond what the tests exercise.

issues.evidence:
%{timer: ref, first_at: at} ->
          Process.cancel_timer(ref)
          at
issues.why: On the coalescing reset path the return of `Process.cancel_timer/1` is ignored and no stale `{:fire, key}` is flushed; if a new `call/4` cast is processed in the instant after the timer has already expired (message already enqueued), the still-present stale `{:fire, key}` is later handled and pops+runs the freshly-installed entry via `run(func)`, firing the newest func earlier than its own delay window. The prompt explicitly asks that ordering/concurrency be handled 'the way the prompt implies', and the harness never exercises this expiry-instant race.

issues.evidence:
%{timer: ref, first_at: at} ->
          Process.cancel_timer(ref)
          at

        nil ->
          now
      end

    remaining_until_max = max(0, first_at + max_ms - now)
    fire_in = max(0, min(delay_ms, remaining_until_max))
    ref = Process.send_after(self(), {:fire, key}, fire_in)

    entry = %{timer: ref, func: func, first_at: first_at}
    {:noreply, Map.put(state, key, entry)}
  end

  ...

  def handle_info({:fire, key}, state) do
    case Map.pop(state, key) do
      {%{func: func}, new_state} ->
        run(func)
        {:noreply, new_state}
issues.why: The `{:fire, key}` message carries no tag/ref tied to the specific timer that scheduled it, and `handle_cast`'s call to `Process.cancel_timer(ref)` ignores its return value. If the old timer has already fired (message already placed in the GenServer's mailbox, e.g. because a prior call resolved to `fire_in = 0` under a tight burst with the API-legal `delay_ms = 0`) at the moment a new `call/4` cast is processed, `cancel_timer` returns `false` but the code proceeds to install a brand-new timer/func entry anyway; the stale `{:fire, key}` message is then delivered next and `handle_info` fires the *new* func immediately (via `Map.pop`, with no ref check), before its own `delay_ms`/`max_ms` window elapses, and the genuinely-scheduled new timer later finds the key already popped and silently does nothing. This breaks the documented guarantee that 'calling again with the same key before its timer fires resets the timer (from delay_ms)' — the func can run far earlier than any caller-specified delay implies — and the deterministic, Process.sleep-paced harness cannot exercise this ordering race, so it is invisible to the passing suite.

issues.evidence:
defp ensure_timer(%{timer: nil} = state), do: start_timer(state)
  defp ensure_timer(state), do: state
issues.why: The prompt states interval elapsing is measured "since the last flush (or since start)", but the timer is only started when a push transitions the buffer from empty to non-empty (in `handle_cast({:push, ...})` via `ensure_timer`), not at process start or at the moment of a flush. If a meaningful delay occurs between start/flush and the next push, the effective flush deadline is push_time + interval_ms rather than flush_time + interval_ms (or start + interval_ms), a divergent-but-plausible interpretation that the harness never distinguishes because every test pushes immediately after start or after a flush.

issues.evidence:
defp dedup_trailing_consonant(word) when byte_size(word) >= 3 do
    len = byte_size(word)
    last = String.at(word, len - 1)
    second_last = String.at(word, len - 2)

    if last == second_last and last not in ~w(a e i o u),
      do: String.slice(word, 0, len - 1),
      else: word
  end
issues.why: This step runs unconditionally at the end of every stem/1 call, even when no suffix from the prompt's list (-ing/-ed/-s/-ly/-tion/-ment) was actually stripped, so any indexed word that merely happens to end in a doubled non-vowel consonant is silently mangled — e.g. "off" stems to "of", "add" to "ad", "all" to "al" — conflating them with unrelated real words already in the vocabulary. The prompt only asked for a 'suffix-stripping stemmer' handling a specific suffix list; this unrequested consonant-doubling normalization introduces incorrect term identity under `stem: true` that no test exercises or catches.

issues.evidence:
test "insert and query returns sorted points within inclusive range", %{db: db} do
    :ok = CounterTSDB.insert(db, "reqs", %{"i" => "a"}, 300, 30)
    :ok = CounterTSDB.insert(db, "reqs", %{"i" => "a"}, 100, 10)
    :ok = CounterTSDB.insert(db, "reqs", %{"i" => "a"}, 200, 20)

    [{%{"i" => "a"}, points}] = CounterTSDB.query(db, "reqs", %{"i" => "a"}, {0, 500})
issues.why: The prompt requires the raw query filter to be inclusive on both ends ("start_ts <= timestamp <= end_ts"), but no test ever inserts a point with timestamp exactly equal to start_ts or end_ts, so a future regression that swapped either `<=` for `<` in the query filter would silently pass the suite.

issues.evidence:
Clock.set(12_000)
    send(db, :cleanup)

    [{_labels, points}] = CounterTSDB.query(db, "reqs", %{}, {0, 20_000})
    assert points == [{5000, 2}]
issues.why: The cleanup spec's removal condition is an exact `<=` ("chunk_start + chunk_duration_ms is less than or equal to now - retention_ms"), but the only cleanup tests use thresholds that are strictly less than or greater than the chunk boundary (1000 <= 2000, 1000 <= 90000), never exactly equal, so a future off-by-one flip from `<=` to `<` in the cleanup predicate would not be caught by the suite.

issues.evidence: raw = base * Integer.pow(2, attempt - 1)
issues.why: The prompt specifies the delay literally as `min(base_delay_ms * 2^attempt, max_delay_ms)` and separately defines the retry attempt counter as 1-based ("attempt is 1-based, counting from the first retry"), which would make the first retry delay `base * 2`; the gold uses `2^(attempt - 1)` so the first retry waits exactly `base`, and the harness cements this ("default base_delay_ms of 100 gives gaps of 100 + 200 + 400 = 700 ms"). A student following the prompt's stated formula and attempt numbering would produce a different, failing backoff schedule.

issues.evidence:
rescue
          exception -> {:error, {:exception, exception}}
        end

      send(parent, {:task_result, key, result})
issues.why: `rescue` only covers raises; if `func` throws or exits, the Task dies before `send/2`, so no `{:task_result, ...}` ever arrives, the key entry is never cleared, and every caller blocked in `GenServer.call(..., :infinity)` hangs forever while the key is permanently poisoned against fresh executions. The harness never exercises a throwing or exiting `func`, so this failure path is unpinned.

The `pool` argument the prompt specifies is discarded and results are delivered only to the process that called `submit` (`{from_pid, _}` is captured as the client), so `await/3` silently returns `{:error, :timeout}` whenever the awaiting process is not the submitting one — a constraint the prompt never states and the harness never exercises, since every test submits and awaits from the same test process.

issues.evidence:
handle_info({:task_finished, worker, ref, result}, state) do
    case Map.get(state.busy_workers, worker) do
      {^ref, client_pid} ->
        send(client_pid, {ref, :result, result})
issues.why: `:task_finished` is never checked against `cancelled_refs`, so if a worker's completion message is already in the manager mailbox when `{:cancel, ref}` is processed, the client is sent both `{ref, :error, :cancelled}` and `{ref, :result, result}`, `cancelled_count` is incremented for a task that actually completed, and the dead worker is briefly re-added to `idle_workers` by `dispatch_next`.

issues.evidence:
# Kill the worker — this will trigger :DOWN
            Process.exit(worker_pid, :kill)
issues.why: The cancelled worker is left in `busy_workers` until the asynchronous `:DOWN` arrives, so a second `cancel/2` for the same running ref still matches in `find_busy_worker` and returns `:ok` with another `cancelled_count` increment instead of the `{:error, :not_found}` the prompt requires for an already-cancelled ref; the harness only exercises double-cancel on a *queued* task ("double cancel returns not_found on second attempt"), so this path is unpinned.

issues.evidence:
{worker_pid, {^ref, client_pid}} ->
            # Mark this ref as cancelled so the :DOWN handler knows
            new_cancelled = MapSet.put(state.cancelled_refs, ref)
            # Kill the worker — this will trigger :DOWN
            Process.exit(worker_pid, :kill)

            # Send cancelled message to the client
            send(client_pid, {ref, :error, :cancelled})

            new_state = %{
              state
              | cancelled_refs: new_cancelled,
                cancelled_count: state.cancelled_count + 1
            }

            {:reply, :ok, new_state}
issues.why: This branch never removes the worker's entry from `state.busy_workers` — cleanup only happens later in the `:DOWN` handler. If a second `cancel(pool, ref)` call for the same ref is processed before that async `:DOWN` message arrives (a real race, since `Process.exit` is fire-and-forget and the reply is sent before the monitor fires), `find_busy_worker` matches the same stale entry again, so the second call also returns `:ok` (double-incrementing `cancelled_count` and sending a second `{ref, :error, :cancelled}`) instead of the `{:error, :not_found}` the prompt requires for an already-cancelled ref ("If the ref is unknown (already completed, already cancelled, or never existed), return `{:error, :not_found}`"), corrupting the cumulative `cancelled_count` reported by `status/1`.

issues.evidence:
timer = schedule_check(name, service.interval_ms)
        new_state = put_in(state.services[name], %{new_service | timer: timer})
issues.why: A manually-driven `{:check, name}` (a documented part of the contract: "Sending the server the message `{:check, service_name}` performs one check") arms a NEW timer while the registration's existing pending timer is never cancelled and its ref is overwritten, so the check chain silently forks — the service is thereafter checked at a doubled cadence, contradicting the prompt's "no doubled cadence" invariant.

issues.evidence:
Process.cancel_timer(service.timer)

        Logger.error("[JsonlIngestion] Batch timed out")
        acc
issues.why: A batch killed by the `:timeout` option is counted in neither `:inserted` nor `:failed`, so its records silently vanish from the stats map and `total` no longer reconciles with `inserted + skipped + failed`; the caller still gets `{:ok, stats}` and cannot tell that N records were dropped. Sequential mode has no such hole, so identical input yields different accounting depending on `:max_concurrency`.

issues.evidence:
conflict_target: [:event_id],
               batch_size: 5,
               max_concurrency: 3
issues.why: The only parallel test uses the default 30s timeout with tiny batches, so no test ever exercises the `:timeout`/`{:exit, :timeout}` path the prompt explicitly introduces; the dropped-record accounting bug above is completely unpinned, as is any failing batch under `max_concurrency > 1` (the failure test runs sequentially only).

issues.evidence:
Unlike a JSON-array ingestion approach, this module never loads the full file
  into memory.
issues.why: The pipeline does `|> Enum.reduce({[], 0, 0}, ...)` and then `batches = Enum.chunk_every(parsed_records, cfg.batch_size)`, materialising every parsed record in a list before any insert, so the documented claim is false and a student learns that `File.stream!` + `Enum.reduce` + `Enum.chunk_every` constitutes a bounded-memory streaming ingest when it does not.

issues.evidence: batches = Enum.chunk_every(parsed_records, cfg.batch_size)
issues.why: `:batch_size` is taken straight from opts with no validation, so `batch_size: 0` raises an ArgumentError out of `ingest/4`, breaking the prompt's "must handle these error conditions gracefully — never raise" contract for a degenerate but plausible option value.

issues.evidence: Use `require Logger` and emit a `Logger.info/1` line after every batch with the running totals.
issues.why: In both `do_insert_batch/5` (sequential) and `insert_parallel/5` (parallel), the failure/timeout branches only call `Logger.error` and never `Logger.info` with running totals (e.g. solution.ex's `do_insert_batch` rescue clause logs only `Logger.error(...)` before returning `%{acc | failed: acc.failed + batch_size}`, and the parallel `{:exit, :timeout}` branch and `{:ok, {:error, batch_size}}` branch likewise emit no Logger.info). A student would learn that per-batch info logging with running totals is optional on failure, contradicting the explicit 'after every batch' requirement.

issues.evidence:
{:exit, :timeout}, acc ->
        Logger.error("[JsonlIngestion] Batch timed out")
        acc
issues.why: On a per-batch timeout in parallel mode, the accumulator is returned unchanged, so the timed-out batch's records are not counted as `:inserted`, `:skipped`, or `:failed` — they silently vanish from the returned stats even though `total` already counted them, breaking the implicit total=inserted+skipped+failed invariant. `:timeout` is a documented option in prompt.md but no test in test_harness.exs ever exercises a batch timeout, so this behavior is unverified and the accounting gap goes unnoticed.

issues.evidence:
by_schema =
      groups
      |> Enum.reduce(%{}, fn {schema, schema_records}, acc ->
        schema_stats = insert_schema_group(repo, schema, schema_records, cfg)
issues.why: The prompt requires "Groups are processed in the order of their first appearance.", but `groups` is a plain map keyed by schema module and `Enum.reduce` over a map iterates in unspecified (term-sorted for small maps) key order, not first-appearance order — so with routing `%{"refund" => Refund, "order" => Order}` and a file whose first record is an order, the Refund group can be inserted and logged before the Order group. The harness only pins within-group ordering (`assert order_ids == ["o-a", "o-b", "o-c", "o-d"]`), so the stated cross-group requirement is silently unmet.

issues.evidence: Logger.warning("[MultiSchemaIngestion] Unknown type '#{type_value}', skipping")
issues.why: The prompt says the module "must handle these error conditions gracefully — never raise", including unroutable types, yet interpolating `type_value` raises `Protocol.UndefinedError` when the discriminator is a JSON object (e.g. `{"type": {"a": 1}}`), aborting the whole ingest instead of counting the record as `:unroutable` and continuing.

issues.evidence:
defp validate_list(value) when is_list(value), do: {:ok, value}
... case Map.fetch(record, type_field) do
issues.why: The prompt states 'The module must handle these error conditions gracefully — never raise'. validate_list only checks that the top-level JSON value is a list; it does not check that each element is a JSON object. If the array contains a non-object element (e.g. a bare string/number/null mixed in among real records), process_records calls Map.fetch(record, type_field) on that element, which raises BadMapError/FunctionClauseError, crashing ingest/4 instead of counting the record and continuing — a genuine violation of the stated never-raise contract for a class of malformed input the harness never exercises.

issues.evidence:

issues.why: The prompt states 'Groups are processed in the order of their first appearance.' `groups` is a plain Elixir map built via Map.update, and Enum.reduce over a map iterates in Erlang term order (roughly alphabetical by module name), not insertion/first-appearance order. With 3+ routing types whose alphabetical order diverges from file-appearance order, processing order (and thus the interleaving of the required per-batch Logger.info lines) would silently diverge from the documented contract; the two-schema, alphabetically-ordered-by-coincidence test fixtures cannot detect this.

issues.evidence:

    # consistent with a pure functional, side-effect-free contract.
    left_index = index_by(left, key_fields)
issues.why: When either input list contains two records sharing the same composite key, `Map.new/2` silently discards all but the last one, so records vanish from every output bucket; the harness has no duplicate-key test, so this lossy policy is neither pinned nor contrasted with any alternative a student might reasonably infer.

issues.evidence:
# Index both sides by composite key — last write wins for duplicate keys,
    # consistent with a pure functional, side-effect-free contract.
issues.why: If `left` or `right` contains multiple records sharing the same composite key (a plausible real-world reconciliation scenario, e.g. duplicate rows), `index_by`/`Map.new` silently drops all but the last record for that key with no error, warning, or documentation in the public @doc — a student could learn this silent-drop behavior as correct, and no test in test_harness.exs exercises duplicate keys within a single list to pin or reveal this choice.

issues.evidence:
defp group_by_key(records, key_fields) do
    Enum.reduce(records, %{}, fn record, acc ->
      key = composite_key(record, key_fields)
      Map.update(acc, key, [record], &[record | &1])
    end)
  end
issues.why: The prompt's Constraints section states 'Key matching is exact: values must be `==` on every key field,' but grouping uses the composite-key tuple as a literal Map key, and Elixir/Erlang maps compare keys with strict term equality, not `==` — so an integer 1 and a float 1.0 are different map keys even though `1 == 1.0` is true. A left record with `id: 1` and a right record with `id: 1.0` would therefore be wrongly reported as only_in_left/only_in_right instead of one_to_one, contradicting the spec's explicit matching rule.

    right = [%{id: 1, name: "Alice"}, %{id: 2, name: "Bob"}]
issues.why: Every test uses same-type key values (plain integers); none exercise a key field whose left/right values are `==` but not identical in representation (e.g. integer vs float, as in `1` vs `1.0`), so the Map-key strict-equality bug described above is never pinned by the harness and would ship silently.

issues.evidence:
test "orphans are discarded by default" do
    items = [
      %{id: 1, parent_id: nil},
      # 99 does not exist
      %{id: 2, parent_id: 99}
    ]

    assert {:ok, roots} = TreeBuilder.build(items)
    all_ids = collect_ids(roots)
    assert 1 in all_ids
    refute 2 in all_ids
  end
issues.why: The gold cascades discard: when an orphan is dropped under the default :discard strategy, its whole subtree becomes unreachable from any root and silently disappears too (build_subtree is never invoked with the orphan's id). The prompt only says 'silently drop orphan nodes' and never states whether descendants of a discarded orphan should also vanish, and no test exercises an orphan with children under :discard (only the :raise_to_root variant is tested for that shape), so this behavioral choice in the gold is entirely unpinned by the harness — a differently-behaving implementation (e.g. one that re-parents or preserves orphan children some other way) would pass all tests just as well.

issues.evidence: {:error, {:duplicate_ids, dupes}}
issues.why: The prompt specifies only `{:error, {:cycle_detected, ids}}` as an error return, but the gold adds an unrequested duplicate-id error path that runs before cycle detection (`case detect_duplicate_ids(items) do`), so a list containing both duplicates and a cycle returns `{:duplicate_ids, ...}` instead of the specified `{:cycle_detected, ...}`; the harness never exercises this branch, so a student could wrongly learn it as required API surface.

issues.evidence: {:error, {:duplicate_ids, dupes}}
issues.why: The prompt only documents two error shapes (`{:error, {:cycle_detected, ids}}` from `build/2` and `{:error, :not_found}` from `subtree/2`) and never mentions duplicate-id handling; the gold silently adds a third, undocumented error contract (`{:duplicate_ids, dupes}`) that the harness never exercises, so a student could wrongly infer this is part of the required public API when it is untested, unspecified behavior.

`missing` is collected over the raw input before `dedup/1` and is never de-duplicated (unlike `dups |> Enum.reverse() |> Enum.uniq()`), so an input such as `[%{id: 1}, %{id: 1}]` yields `%{type: :missing_parent_id, ids: [1, 1]}` — a repeated id naming a node that was already dropped as a duplicate; no harness test combines duplicate ids with an absent `:parent_id` key, so this boundary is unpinned.

prompt.md requires duplicate_id's `ids` to be ordered 'in first-seen order' (order of each id's FIRST appearance), but dedup/1 accumulates `dups` in the order the *second* occurrence is encountered. For input order id=B, id=A, id=A(dup), id=B(dup) — B is first-seen at position 1 and A at position 2 — the code yields `dup_ids = [A, B]` instead of the spec-correct `[B, A]`, silently teaching a wrong ordering rule for a case the tests never exercise (all existing duplicate tests use only one duplicated id).

issues.evidence:
{normalized, missing_pid_ids} = normalize(items)
    {deduped, dup_ids} = dedup(normalized)
issues.why: normalize/1 (which builds missing_pid_ids by checking `Map.has_key?(item, :parent_id)`) runs on the raw item list before dedup/1 drops later duplicate-id entries. If the surviving (first) occurrence of an id has an explicit valid parent_id but a later, dropped duplicate of the same id lacks the :parent_id key, the id still lands in missing_pid_ids. E.g. items = [%{id:1,parent_id:2}, %{id:1}, %{id:2,parent_id:nil}] produces forest where node 1 is nested as a CHILD of node 2, yet issues includes %{type: :missing_parent_id, ids: [1]} — contradicting prompt.md's documented repair 'treat each as a root' for an id whose surviving node was never rooted that way. This is an internally inconsistent diagnostic a student would trust as accurate.

issues.evidence:
test "multiple issue types are ordered dup, missing_parent, orphan, cycle" do
    items = [
      %{id: 1, parent_id: nil},

The two behavioral bugs above (duplicate ordering, missing_parent_id/dedup contradiction) both live exactly in this untested overlap, so the harness gives no signal on the scenario the prompt calls out by name.

issues.evidence:
# Key only exists in base-less territory and is not locked — take it.
        not Map.has_key?(base, key) ->
          Map.put(acc, key, Map.fetch!(override, key))
issues.why: When an *ancestor* of a locked path is missing from the base, the whole override subtree is copied verbatim without ever consulting `locked?/2`, so the locked key is injected — e.g. `merge(%{}, %{db: %{password: "pwned"}}, locked: [[:db, :password]])` returns `%{db: %{password: "pwned"}}`, directly contradicting the prompt's "If the base does not define a locked key, the override cannot inject it: that key is simply absent from the result." The same hole applies to `:list_strategies`, which is likewise never consulted along a copied subtree.

Every non-injection test gives the base the parent map (`%{db: ...}`, `%{other: "base"}`), so the boundary where the locked path's *parent* is absent from the base is never exercised; the harness reports green on a gold that leaks locked secrets in exactly that case, teaching students the wholesale-copy shortcut is safe.

issues.evidence: defp leaf_provenance(_value, name, path, pr), do: Map.put(pr, path, name)
issues.why: Under `list_strategy: :append`, a list leaf that only one layer ever sets gets provenance as a bare layer name (seeded by `leaf_provenance`/the new-key branch `{Map.put(acc, k, ov), leaf_provenance(ov, name, kpath, pr)}`), not the "list of layer names ... that contributed elements" the prompt specifies; the value type of a provenance entry therefore silently changes with the number of contributors, so consumers must handle both `:base` and `[:base, :env]` at the same path.

issues.evidence: assert List.wrap(result.provenance[[:plugins]]) == [:base]
issues.why: The harness deliberately normalises through `List.wrap/1` ("a lone contributor is accepted either as its bare name or as a one-element list of names"), so the single-contributor append boundary — the one case where the gold's provenance shape diverges from the prompt — is left unpinned and a regression in either direction would pass.

The prompt says a locked path 'already exists in a lower-precedence layer, higher layers must not change it'. But this final branch of merge_value fires whenever a higher layer replaces an entire map subtree with a scalar/list, and it unconditionally overwrites everything beneath kpath (via prune_subtree) without ever consulting opts.locked_paths for the descendant paths being wiped out. Example: base = %{db: %{password: "x", host: "h"}}, higher layer = %{db: "disabled"}, locked: [[:db, :password]] — the locked password is silently destroyed (config.db becomes "disabled") even though [:db] itself isn't locked, only [:db, :password] is; the lock check `locked?(kpath, opts) and Map.has_key?(base_map, k)` in merge_map is only ever evaluated at the exact key being iterated ([:db]), never for the nested locked descendant, so the 'must not change it' guarantee silently fails for this interaction.

issues.evidence: test "locked nested key is preserved while siblings merge" ... test "provenance drops paths whose subtree a higher layer replaced with a scalar"
issues.why: The harness tests locked-nested-key preservation and subtree-replaced-by-leaf pruning as two separate scenarios, but never combines them (a locked descendant path whose ancestor subtree is replaced wholesale by a higher-precedence scalar/list). This is exactly the boundary implied by combining the prompt's 'Locked keys' and 'Subtree replaced by a leaf' rules, and the gold's actual behavior there (silently discarding the locked value, see the logical_correctness finding) goes completely unpinned by the suite.

issues.evidence:
is_list(bv) and is_list(ov) ->
        case list_strategy_for(kpath, opts) do
          :replace -> ov
          :append -> bv ++ ov
        end

      true ->
        ov
    end
  end
issues.why: When the accumulated value and the override value have mismatched shapes the `true -> ov` fallback copies the override subtree wholesale without any locked-path stripping, so a layer can both introduce and erase locked paths that the dedicated branches otherwise protect: with `base: %{db: nil}, locked: [[:db, :password]]` a layer `%{db: %{password: "pwned"}}` makes `get(s, [:db, :password])` return `"pwned"`, contradicting "If the base does not define a locked path, no layer may introduce it — the effective value there stays absent"; symmetrically `base: %{db: %{password: "s3cr3t"}}` plus a layer `%{db: "x"}` discards the locked base value. The harness only exercises map/map and scalar/scalar pairs at locked paths, so the hole is unpinned and a student would learn the invariant as airtight.

The only list-strategy test is the global `:append` case; the per-path `:list_strategies` lookup in the gold (`Map.get(per_key, kpath, global)`, plus its tuple normalisation via `Map.new(fn {path, strat} -> {normalise_path(path), strat} end)`) is never exercised, so a broken or ignored per-path override — including tuple-form keys that the prompt explicitly says must be accepted — would pass the suite unnoticed.

prompt.md explicitly references a distinct `:list_strategies`-style option ("Key paths in `:locked`, `:required`, and `:list_strategies`-style options are lists or tuples of atoms."), and solution.ex implements a whole dedicated code path for it (`per_key = opts |> Keyword.get(:list_strategies, %{}) |> Map.new(fn {path, strat} -> {normalise_path(path), strat} end)` plus `list_strategy_for/2`'s `Map.get(per_key, kpath, global)` lookup), but the harness never passes a `:list_strategies` map in any test — only the singular global `:list_strategy` is exercised. A broken per-key override (wrong precedence, broken path normalisation, wrong default fallback) would pass the full suite undetected.

issues.evidence: Because a direct ETS read can race with a concurrent eviction, the touch path must tolerate the key having disappeared between the read and the ordering update: in that case it simply does nothing and the caller still gets its `{:ok, value}`. The server must never crash on this race.
issues.why: The gold's `handle_call({:touch, key}, ...)` correctly handles the vanished-key case (the `[] -> {:reply, :ok, state}` branch in solution.ex), but test_harness.exs contains no test that exercises this path — there is no test that simulates or forces the key to disappear between the ETS read in `get/2` and the `{:touch, key}` call (e.g. by deleting the row from the data table before invoking the touch message, or by racing a concurrent evicting `put`). This explicitly-called-out crash-safety requirement is therefore implemented but left completely unpinned by the harness, so a regression here (e.g. a future edit that removes the `[]` clause and crashes on `MatchError`) would pass the suite undetected.

Because the quantifier has no boundary assertion, the greedy match can cross from the end of one digit run into the start of an adjacent one when they're separated by only a single space or hyphen (e.g. "4111111111111111 4222222222222222"): the engine will happily consume all 16 digits of the first number plus the separator plus several digits of the second number (up to the 19-digit cap) as a single 'credit card' match. This produces a scrubbed output that mixes unmasked digits from the second number into what looks like the tail of the first, and miscounts/mismasks two distinct numbers as one (plus a stray leftover match), which is a real soundness gap the harness's single-card test never exercises.

The prompt defines the credit-card pattern as 'any sequence of 13–19 digits' — 19 is an explicit upper limit — but the regex has no lookaround to reject longer runs, so a 20+ digit identifier (tracking number, account ID, etc.) still gets a false-positive match on its first 19 digits and is partially masked/reported as a credit card. This is exactly the kind of 'limits' boundary the rubric calls out, and the harness only ever tests a single in-range 16-digit example, so the boundary is never pinned.

  defp ensure_timer(%{timer: nil} = state), do: start_timer(state)
  defp ensure_timer(state), do: state
issues.why: The prompt defines the interval as measured from the flush/start ("`:interval_ms` — ... If this much time passes since the last flush (or since start) while events are buffered, flush them" and "The next time-based flush must happen a full `:interval_ms` after the most recent flush"), but the gold cancels the timer on flush and only restarts it on the next push, so an event pushed late in a window waits up to nearly 2×`interval_ms` instead of firing `interval_ms` after the previous flush. Every harness timing test pushes immediately after start or immediately after a flush (`WeightedAggregator.push(agg, 5)` right after `assert_receive {:flushed, [10, 95]}`), so the two readings are never distinguished and a student could learn the deferred-timer semantics as the specified behavior.

The `gen` tag is minted once per batch (`start_batch`), not per timer arming, so a `{:idle_flush, gen}` message that was already delivered to the mailbox before `Process.cancel_timer/1` ran (cancel returns `false` in that case, and the message is not flushed) still matches `handle_info({:idle_flush, gen}, %{gen: gen} = state)` for the same batch. If a push is processed in the same window in which the old idle timer expires, the batch flushes immediately instead of after a fresh `:idle_ms`, violating requirement 2 ("Each push resets the idle timer to a fresh `:idle_ms`"); the race is narrow and untested by the harness, so students learn a debounce reset that is not actually leak-proof.

issues.why: When Kahn's algorithm stalls, the gold reports EVERY task still unresolved as part of the cycle, not just the tasks actually forming it. A caller trying to fix the reported cycle by inspecting z's dependency declaration would be misled.

issues.why: Execution is layer-synchronous: every task in layer N+1 waits for the slowest task of layer N even when its own dependencies already finished, so in a graph like `a` (slow, no deps) + `c -> d` the ready task `d` is held back by unrelated `a`, contradicting "Independent tasks that are all ready and do not depend on one another run in parallel". issues.why: Execution proceeds strictly layer-by-layer (Kahn's BFS layering) and awaits every task in a layer before starting the next layer, so a ready task can be delayed by an unrelated, slower task that merely happens to share its layer. Example: submit A (no deps, sleeps 100ms), B (depends_on: [:a], instant), and unrelated C (no deps, sleeps 500ms). Kahn's algorithm puts A and C in layer 0 and B alone in layer 1, so B cannot start until C finishes at 500ms even though B has zero relationship to C — contradicting prompt.md's 'Independent tasks that are all ready and do not depend on one another run **in parallel**' and the stated design goal 'real parallelism of independent ready tasks.' No test in test_harness.exs constructs a same-layer-but-unrelated slow task, so this false synchronization barrier is never caught.

issues.why: The tolerance is absolute (`@epsilon 1.0e-9`) while `target = percentile * total` scales with the decayed total weight, so once a series has aged enough that its total weight drops below ~1e-9 (e.g. half_life_ms 1_000 and ~31 seconds of idle time: 0.5^31 ≈ 4.7e-10 per sample) `target - @epsilon` is negative and the reduce halts on the very first sorted element, returning the minimum sample for every percentile. This silently breaks the two guarantees the prompt states explicitly — "`p = 1.0` returns the maximum" and "**Uniform aging is neutral**: if no new samples arrive, advancing the clock scales every weight by the same factor, so the reported percentile is unchanged" — since merely advancing the clock flips p95/p100 from the max to the min without any underflow-to-zero having occurred.

case strip_one_newline(content) do
      "" -> []
      stripped -> parse_chars(stripped, "", false, false, [], [])
    end
issues.why: No test ever passes an empty (or newline-only) CSV, so the `"" -> []` branch — the only guard against a wholly-empty input producing a single spurious record `[{"", false}]` — is never executed; a variant that dropped it would still pass the suite while `infer_string("")` silently returned `%{"" => :string}` instead of `%{}`.

issues.evidence: otherwise `boolean` (`true`/`false`, case-insensitive), `integer` (`^[+-]?\d+$`), `float` (`^[+-]?\d+\.\d+$`), `date` (valid `YYYY-MM-DD` or `MM/DD/YYYY`)
issues.why: The gold correctly handles the signed-number (`[+-]?`), case-insensitive boolean, and calendar-validity (`Date.new`/`NaiveDateTime.new` rejecting e.g. `2020-13-45`) boundaries, but no test in the harness exercises any of them — the classification-boundary half of the spec is unpinned, so a variant that dropped the sign class or the date-validity check would still pass.

The prompt restricts the collapse rule to "a doubled letter that is not a vowel", but the guard only excludes the five vowels, so doubled digits are collapsed too — with `stem: true` the token "2022" is stored as "202" and "1900" as "190", silently colliding with genuinely distinct numeric tokens (the tokenizer explicitly keeps 0-9 via `~r/[^a-z0-9]+/`). No harness test combines digits with stemming, so the divergence is unpinned.

issues.evidence:
defp strip_prefix(str, prefix) do
    String.replace_prefix(str, prefix, "")
  end
issues.why: `parse_commit` does `lines = String.split(content, "\n", parts: 4)` and then `strip_prefix(Enum.at(lines, 1), "parent ")`; for a stored object with fewer than 4 lines (any blob such as `"hello world"`, or a 1–3 entry tree) `Enum.at/2` yields `nil` and `String.replace_prefix/3` raises FunctionClauseError. Since the prompt explicitly puts blobs, trees and commits in one flat namespace, `log(server, blob_or_tree_hash)` — a hash that *is* found — crashes the GenServer and, because `start_link` links the caller, takes the caller down with it instead of returning a value; the harness never passes a non-commit hash to `log`, so this failure path is unpinned.

issues.evidence:
def handle_call({:commit, tree, parents, message, author}, _from, state) do
    object = build_commit_object(tree, parents, message, author)
    hash = sha1_hex(object)
    objects = Map.put_new(state.objects, hash, object)
    {:reply, {:ok, hash}, %{state | objects: objects}}
  end
...
%{parents: parents} = parse_commit(Map.fetch!(objects, node))
issues.why: commit/5 never checks that tree_hash or any parent hash already exists in the store, so a caller can create a commit whose parent references a hash nothing was ever stored under; log/2 and merge_base/3 then walk that dangling parent via Map.fetch!(objects, node), which raises a KeyError (crashing the GenServer) instead of returning a graceful {:error, ...} the way retrieve/2 and the documented not_found paths do elsewhere. No harness test exercises this, so the gap is unpinned by execution.

issues.evidence:
defp object_path(dir, hash) do
    <<prefix::binary-size(2), rest::binary>> = hash
    Path.join([dir, prefix, rest])
  end
issues.why: A hash string shorter than two bytes (e.g. `retrieve(server, "")` or `has_object?(server, "a")`) raises MatchError inside the GenServer callback, crashing the store for all clients instead of returning `{:error, :not_found}` / `false` as the prompt's "If no file exists for that hash it returns {:error, :not_found}" implies; the harness never passes a malformed hash so nothing pins this.

issues.evidence:
defp bucket_dir(state, bucket), do: Path.join(state.buckets_dir, bucket)

      not File.dir?(bucket_path) ->
        {:reply, {:error, :not_found}, state}

      not bucket_empty?(state, name) ->
        {:reply, {:error, :not_empty}, state}

      true ->
        File.rm_rf!(bucket_path)
issues.why: Bucket names are joined into a path with no validation on the delete path, and the name charset the prompt itself declares valid includes dots, so `delete_bucket(server, "..")` resolves to `root_dir`, finds no `objects` subdirectory (`bucket_empty?` returns true on `{:error, :enoent}`), and recursively deletes the entire storage root while returning `:ok` instead of the prompt-specified `{:error, :not_found}`; likewise `create_bucket(server, ".")` returns `{:error, :already_exists}` for a name the prompt says is legal.

issues.evidence:
defp persist_bucket(root, name, keys) do
    path = Path.join(root, name <> @bucket_suffix)
...
    name = String.replace_suffix(file, @bucket_suffix, "")
issues.why: The bucket→filename mapping is not injective for names the prompt calls valid: a bucket named "." (dots are explicitly allowed) is written to the file ".bin", and on reload `String.replace_suffix("​.bin", ".bin", "")` yields the empty string, so after a restart the bucket is registered under "" — its versions become unreachable and `create_bucket(server, ".")` returns `:ok` again instead of the `{:error, :already_exists}` the Persistence section mandates.

issues.evidence: @name_regex ~r/^[a-z0-9.\-]+$/
issues.why: In PCRE `$` also matches immediately before a trailing newline, so `"my-bucket\n"` passes `valid_name?/1` even though it contains a character outside the allowed lowercase-alphanumeric/hyphen/dot set; the store silently accepts (and creates a file for) a bucket name the prompt says must yield `{:error, :invalid_name}`, and the harness never probes a trailing newline.

issues.evidence: assert [] = TSDB.query_agg(db, "nonexistent", %{}, {0, 1000}, :sum, 500)
issues.why: This is the only place the harness checks the "series dropped when all its windows are omitted" contract from the prompt ("A matched series whose windows all get omitted is itself dropped from the result"), and here nothing matches at all; the gold's `if agg_points == [] do [] else [{labels, agg_points}]` branch is never exercised by a series that matches labels and has points in range but yields zero windows (e.g. `:rate` with a single in-range point), so a solution returning `{labels, []}` in that case would still pass.

No raw-query test ever places a point exactly at `start_ts` or `end_ts` (all query bounds are strictly outside the sample timestamps), so the prompt's inclusive filter `start_ts <= timestamp <= end_ts` — implemented in the gold as `ts >= start_ts and ts <= end_ts` — is unpinned; a submission using an exclusive upper bound would pass the whole suite while silently dropping boundary samples.
